skills.name: zoning-analysis
skills.skill_md:
# Zoning & Entitlement Analysis

You are performing a zoning and entitlement deep-dive for a real estate development site. The goal is to give a developer a clear, actionable picture of what they can build on a specific parcel — by right, with conditional approval, and what's outright prohibited — along with the procedural steps and dimensional constraints that will shape their site plan.

This is one module in a broader development underwriting workflow. Your output here feeds directly into density calculations, unit mix planning, and cost estimation. Precision matters because zoning misreads can kill deals or lead to months of wasted entitlement effort.

## Inputs

The user will typically provide one or more of:

- A property address
- A broker listing URL or offering memorandum (OM)
- A parcel ID or APN
- A zoning designation (e.g., "R-4 SD", "PUD-M", "C-2 with MF overlay")
- A county or municipality name

Your job is to work with whatever they give you and fill in the rest.

## Research Workflow

Follow these steps in order. Each step builds on the prior one. If you hit a dead end on one source, try alternatives before moving on.

### Step 1: Identify the Property and Jurisdiction

**Goal:** Confirm the exact parcel location, acreage, and governing jurisdiction (county vs. municipality — this determines which zoning ordinance applies).

**Actions:**
- If the user provided a listing URL, extract the property details first (address, acreage, listed zoning, asking price) using browser tools or web search
- Use web search to confirm the county and whether the property is in an incorporated municipality or unincorporated county land — the zoning authority differs
- Search for the county/city GIS portal or parcel viewer to confirm parcel boundaries and zoning designation
- Cross-reference the zoning shown on the GIS with what the broker claims — discrepancies happen and matter

**What to capture:**
- Full address
- County and municipality (or "unincorporated")
- Parcel ID / APN if available
- Gross acreage (from GIS, not just the listing)
- Zoning designation as shown on the official zoning map
- Any overlay districts, special districts, or planned development designations

### Step 2: Find the Zoning Ordinance

**Goal:** Locate the actual text of the zoning ordinance that governs this parcel.

**Actions:**
- Search for "[County/City name] zoning ordinance" or "[County/City name] zoning regulations PDF"
- Check the county/city planning department website — most post their ordinance online as a PDF or municipal code chapter
- If the ordinance is hosted on Municode, American Legal, or similar codification services, those are usually accessible
- If the full ordinance is behind a paywall or inaccessible, search for the specific district article (e.g., "Shelby County R-4 zoning district regulations")

**Important:** You need the actual ordinance text, not a summary or a third-party interpretation. Broker OMs and zoning summaries on listing sites are often incomplete or wrong on the details. Always go to the primary source.

### Step 3: Analyze the Base Zoning District

**Goal:** Extract the complete regulatory framework for the base zoning district.

Read the relevant article/section of the zoning ordinance and extract:

**Permitted Uses (By-Right):**
- List every residential use type explicitly permitted: single-family detached, single-family attached, two-family (duplex), townhomes, multifamily apartments, condominiums, manufactured housing, etc.
- Note non-residential uses that might be relevant: commercial, mixed-use, live-work, home occupations
- Pay attention to how uses are categorized — some ordinances distinguish between "apartments" and "multifamily dwellings" or between "townhouses" and "row houses"

**Conditional Uses (CUP/Special Exception):**
- List uses that require a Conditional Use Permit, Special Use Permit, Special Exception, or similar discretionary approval
- Note the approval body (Planning Commission, Board of Zoning Appeals, City Council)
- Describe the approval criteria if stated in the ordinance (these tell you how hard the CUP will be to get)

**Prohibited Uses:**
- Explicitly note any use types the developer is interested in that are NOT listed as permitted or conditional — absence from both lists typically means prohibited

**Dimensional Standards:**
Extract these for each relevant use type (they often differ between single-family, townhome, and multifamily within the same district):

- Minimum lot area (total and per dwelling unit — these are different things)
- Minimum lot width / frontage
- Maximum lot coverage / impervious surface
- Front, side, and rear yard setbacks
- Maximum building height (in feet and/or stories)
- Minimum open space / green space percentage
- Parking requirements (spaces per unit by bedroom count if available)
- Any floor area ratio (FAR) maximums
- Minimum unit size requirements if any

**Density Calculation:**
- Identify the density-limiting standard (usually minimum lot area per dwelling unit or maximum units per acre)
- Note whether it applies to gross or net acreage (most ordinances apply to the full lot/parcel area, not net buildable)
- Calculate the maximum unit count: gross site area ÷ minimum area per unit
- Flag if townhomes and apartments have different density standards

**Critical: Do not double-count site losses.** The zoning density standard (e.g., 3,000 SF/unit) is a legal cap applied to the gross lot area. The physical site losses (roads, parking, stormwater, setbacks, open space, topography) are a *separate* constraint that determines what you can actually fit. Report both independently:
- Zoning ceiling: gross acreage × 43,560 ÷ (SF per unit) = max legal unit count
- Physical feasibility: apply typical units-per-acre ranges by product type to gross acreage, then sanity check against physical constraints

Do NOT multiply the gross acreage by an efficiency factor (e.g., 60%) and then divide by the per-unit minimum — that double-counts the losses and understates the legal ceiling. The zoning standard doesn't care about net buildable area; it cares about the parcel area.

**Example (from Oak Mountain Heights, 24 acres R-4, 3,000 SF/unit):**
- Wrong: 1,045,440 × 0.60 ÷ 3,000 = 209 units (double-counts losses)
- Right: 1,045,440 ÷ 3,000 = 348 unit legal ceiling; physical feasibility check separately confirms ~348 units fits at 14.5 units/acre for garden-style

### Step 4: Analyze Overlay / Special Districts

**Goal:** If the property has an overlay designation (SD, PUD, PD, SPA, TOD, etc.), understand how it modifies the base zoning.

Overlay and special districts typically do one or more of:
- Add permitted uses beyond what the base district allows
- Modify dimensional standards (setbacks, height, density)
- Impose additional procedural requirements (site plan review, design review, traffic studies)
- Grant the planning commission discretionary authority over the development plan

**Key questions to answer:**
- Does the overlay supersede or supplement the base district standards?
- Which dimensional standards from the base district still apply vs. which are subject to planning commission discretion?
- What submissions are required for approval? (site plan, traffic study, drainage plan, market study, environmental assessment, etc.)
- Are there construction timeline requirements? (some SD/PUD designations require commencement within 1-2 years or the designation lapses)
- What is the minimum site size for the overlay to apply?

### Step 5: Synthesize the Development Feasibility Assessment

**Goal:** Translate the raw zoning data into an actionable development analysis.

Structure your output as follows:

**Property Summary:**
- Address, acreage, jurisdiction, zoning designation, parcel ID

**By-Right Development Potential:**
- What product types can be built without discretionary approval
- Maximum density / unit count for each product type
- Key dimensional constraints (height, setbacks, parking)
- Estimated unit range accounting for physical site constraints

**Conditional / Discretionary Approval Path:**
- What additional product types or densities are available with CUP or plan approval
- The approval body and process
- Required submissions and studies
- Timeline and cost implications of the entitlement process
- Political/practical risk factors (adjacent neighborhoods, precedent, planning staff disposition)

**Key Constraints and Risks:**
- Density ceiling and how close the proposed program is to it
- Height restrictions that limit product type (e.g., 35-ft max = 3 stories wood frame, no mid-rise)
- Parking requirements that could constrain density
- Open space / setback requirements that reduce buildable area
- Timeline requirements for SD/PUD designations
- Any moratorium, pending ordinance changes, or known political issues

**What You Could Build (Scenario Summary):**
For each realistic product type, provide:
- Product type and construction type
- Approximate unit count range
- Whether by-right or requires approval
- Key constraint that limits density

**Always consider a mixed-product scenario.** Single-product scenarios (all garden-style, all townhomes, all SFR) are often sub-optimal. Mixed product typically delivers better returns because:
- Higher rent PSF products (mid-rise, townhomes) can subsidize lower-cost land utilization
- Product variety creates multiple exit options and tenant segments
- Phased delivery can derisk absorption
- Overlay/PD districts often explicitly permit mixed product within a single plan

When evaluating a site, propose at least one mixed-product scenario alongside any single-product scenarios — e.g., townhomes + garden + mid-rise, or SFR + townhomes, depending on site topography and market demand profile.

## Important Notes

**Always go to primary sources.** Broker OMs, Zillow, and real estate listing sites often get zoning details wrong — especially overlay districts, conditional uses, and dimensional standards. The county/city ordinance is the source of truth.

**Distinguish between the zoning map and the zoning text.** The map tells you the district designation; the ordinance text tells you what that designation means. You need both.

**Watch for recent amendments.** Zoning ordinances get amended frequently. If the PDF you're reading looks dated, search for recent amendments to the specific article or district.

**Municipal vs. county jurisdiction matters.** A property in an incorporated city is governed by that city's zoning ordinance, not the county's — even if the county GIS shows a zoning designation. Confirm which jurisdiction has zoning authority.

**The SD/PUD trap.** Special district and planned unit development overlays sound developer-friendly because of their flexibility, but they also mean the planning commission has broad discretion to impose conditions. Flexibility cuts both ways. Always flag the discretionary risk.

**Density is not the same as what you can physically build.** The zoning density cap (e.g., 3,000 SF/unit = 14.5 units/acre on gross) is a legal maximum. Physical constraints (topography, stormwater, access, parking, setbacks) separately limit what fits on the site. Report both — the zoning ceiling and a practical estimate.

## Output Format

Present findings in a structured narrative — not a raw data dump. The audience is a developer or acquisitions professional who needs to make a go/no-go decision. Lead with the bottom line (can we build what we want here?), then support it with the regulatory detail.

If the user has specified a target program (e.g., "300 apartments and 100 townhomes"), evaluate that specific program against the zoning and tell them whether it works, what approvals are needed, and where the constraints bite.

## Before You Finalize — Run the Checklist

Before delivering your analysis, review `references/analyst-checklist.md`. It catalogs specific mistakes made on past deals and prompts to slow down and think harder on common failure modes — density math double-counting, source verification, scenario design, Excel model validation, and more. Not every check applies to every deal, but the act of going through the list surfaces issues that are easy to miss when you're moving quickly.

## Expanding This Skill

This skill is designed to grow as you underwrite more deals. After each analysis, consider:
- Did you encounter a zoning structure or ordinance format not covered here? Add a note to `references/zoning-patterns.md`
- Did a particular research technique work well (or fail)? Update the workflow steps
- Are there jurisdiction-specific quirks worth documenting? Add to `references/jurisdiction-notes.md`
- Did you make a mistake Jack had to correct? Add it to `references/analyst-checklist.md` under "Common Mistakes to Avoid" so you don't repeat it
skills.skill_md_full:
---
name: zoning-analysis
description: >
  Zoning and entitlement analysis for real estate development sites. Use this skill whenever the user
  provides a property address, listing URL, or parcel ID and wants to understand what can be built there.
  Triggers include: any mention of zoning, entitlements, permitted uses, conditional use permits (CUP),
  density analysis, setbacks, dimensional standards, by-right development, site plan review, overlay
  districts, special districts, planned unit developments (PUD), or any question about what a zoning
  code allows or restricts. Also trigger when the user sends a broker listing or offering memorandum
  and wants to evaluate development feasibility — zoning is always the first gate. This skill is part
  of a broader development underwriting toolkit and should be used as the first step in any new deal
  analysis.
---

# Zoning & Entitlement Analysis

You are performing a zoning and entitlement deep-dive for a real estate development site. The goal is to give a developer a clear, actionable picture of what they can build on a specific parcel — by right, with conditional approval, and what's outright prohibited — along with the procedural steps and dimensional constraints that will shape their site plan.

This is one module in a broader development underwriting workflow. Your output here feeds directly into density calculations, unit mix planning, and cost estimation. Precision matters because zoning misreads can kill deals or lead to months of wasted entitlement effort.

## Inputs

The user will typically provide one or more of:

- A property address
- A broker listing URL or offering memorandum (OM)
- A parcel ID or APN
- A zoning designation (e.g., "R-4 SD", "PUD-M", "C-2 with MF overlay")
- A county or municipality name

Your job is to work with whatever they give you and fill in the rest.

## Research Workflow

Follow these steps in order. Each step builds on the prior one. If you hit a dead end on one source, try alternatives before moving on.

### Step 1: Identify the Property and Jurisdiction

- Search for the county/city GIS portal or parcel viewer to confirm parcel boundaries and zoning designation
- Cross-reference the zoning shown on the GIS with what the broker claims — discrepancies happen and matter

**What to capture:**
- Full address
- County and municipality (or "unincorporated")

- If the full ordinance is behind a paywall or inaccessible, search for the specific district article (e.g., "Shelby County R-4 zoning district regulations")

**Important:** You need the actual ordinance text, not a summary or a third-party interpretation. Broker OMs and zoning summaries on listing sites are often incomplete or wrong on the details. Always go to the primary source.

### Step 3: Analyze the Base Zoning District

**Goal:** Extract the complete regulatory framework for the base zoning district.

Read the relevant article/section of the zoning ordinance and extract:

**Permitted Uses (By-Right):**
- List every residential use type explicitly permitted: single-family detached, single-family attached, two-family (duplex), townhomes, multifamily apartments, condominiums, manufactured housing, etc.
- Note non-residential uses that might be relevant: commercial, mixed-use, live-work, home occupations
- Pay attention to how uses are categorized — some ordinances distinguish between "apartments" and "multifamily dwellings" or between "townhouses" and "row houses"

**Conditional Uses (CUP/Special Exception):**
- List uses that require a Conditional Use Permit, Special Use Permit, Special Exception, or similar discretionary approval
- Note the approval body (Planning Commission, Board of Zoning Appeals, City Council)
- Describe the approval criteria if stated in the ordinance (these tell you how hard the CUP will be to get)

**Prohibited Uses:**
- Explicitly note any use types the developer is interested in that are NOT listed as permitted or conditional — absence from both lists typically means prohibited

**Dimensional Standards:**
Extract these for each relevant use type (they often differ between single-family, townhome, and multifamily within the same district):

- Minimum lot area (total and per dwelling unit — these are different things)
- Minimum lot width / frontage
- Maximum lot coverage / impervious surface
- Front, side, and rear yard setbacks
- Maximum building height (in feet and/or stories)
- Minimum open space / green space percentage
- Parking requirements (spaces per unit by bedroom count if available)
- Any floor area ratio (FAR) maximums
- Minimum unit size requirements if any

**Density Calculation:**
- Identify the density-limiting standard (usually minimum lot area per dwelling unit or maximum units per acre)
- Note whether it applies to gross or net acreage (most ordinances apply to the full lot/parcel area, not net buildable)
- Calculate the maximum unit count: gross site area ÷ minimum area per unit
- Flag if townhomes and apartments have different density standards

**Critical: Do not double-count site losses.** The zoning density standard (e.g., 3,000 SF/unit) is a legal cap applied to the gross lot area. The physical site losses (roads, parking, stormwater, setbacks, open space, topography) are a *separate* constraint that determines what you can actually fit. Report both independently:
- Zoning ceiling: gross acreage × 43,560 ÷ (SF per unit) = max legal unit count
- Physical feasibility: apply typical units-per-acre ranges by product type to gross acreage, then sanity check against physical constraints

Do NOT multiply the gross acreage by an efficiency factor (e.g., 60%) and then divide by the per-unit minimum — that double-counts the losses and understates the legal ceiling. The zoning standard doesn't care about net buildable area; it cares about the parcel area.

- Right: 1,045,440 ÷ 3,000 = 348 unit legal ceiling; physical feasibility check separately confirms ~348 units fits at 14.5 units/acre for garden-style

### Step 4: Analyze Overlay / Special Districts

**Goal:** If the property has an overlay designation (SD, PUD, PD, SPA, TOD, etc.), understand how it modifies the base zoning.

Overlay and special districts typically do one or more of:
- Add permitted uses beyond what the base district allows
- Modify dimensional standards (setbacks, height, density)
- Impose additional procedural requirements (site plan review, design review, traffic studies)
- Grant the planning commission discretionary authority over the development plan

**Key questions to answer:**
- Does the overlay supersede or supplement the base district standards?
- Which dimensional standards from the base district still apply vs. which are subject to planning commission discretion?
- What submissions are required for approval? (site plan, traffic study, drainage plan, market study, environmental assessment, etc.)
- Are there construction timeline requirements? (some SD/PUD designations require commencement within 1-2 years or the designation lapses)
- What is the minimum site size for the overlay to apply?

### Step 5: Synthesize the Development Feasibility Assessment

**Goal:** Translate the raw zoning data into an actionable development analysis.

Structure your output as follows:

**Property Summary:**
- Address, acreage, jurisdiction, zoning designation, parcel ID

**By-Right Development Potential:**
- What product types can be built without discretionary approval
- Maximum density / unit count for each product type
- Key dimensional constraints (height, setbacks, parking)
- Estimated unit range accounting for physical site constraints

**Conditional / Discretionary Approval Path:**
- What additional product types or densities are available with CUP or plan approval
- The approval body and process
- Required submissions and studies
- Timeline and cost implications of the entitlement process
- Political/practical risk factors (adjacent neighborhoods, precedent, planning staff disposition)

**Key Constraints and Risks:**
- Density ceiling and how close the proposed program is to it
- Height restrictions that limit product type (e.g., 35-ft max = 3 stories wood frame, no mid-rise)
- Parking requirements that could constrain density
- Open space / setback requirements that reduce buildable area
- Timeline requirements for SD/PUD designations
- Any moratorium, pending ordinance changes, or known political issues

**What You Could Build (Scenario Summary):**
For each realistic product type, provide:
- Product type and construction type
- Approximate unit count range
- Whether by-right or requires approval
- Key constraint that limits density

**Always consider a mixed-product scenario.** Single-product scenarios (all garden-style, all townhomes, all SFR) are often sub-optimal. Mixed product typically delivers better returns because:
- Higher rent PSF products (mid-rise, townhomes) can subsidize lower-cost land utilization
- Product variety creates multiple exit options and tenant segments
- Phased delivery can derisk absorption
- Overlay/PD districts often explicitly permit mixed product within a single plan

When evaluating a site, propose at least one mixed-product scenario alongside any single-product scenarios — e.g., townhomes + garden + mid-rise, or SFR + townhomes, depending on site topography and market demand profile.

## Important Notes

**Always go to primary sources.** Broker OMs, Zillow, and real estate listing sites often get zoning details wrong — especially overlay districts, conditional uses, and dimensional standards. The county/city ordinance is the source of truth.

**Distinguish between the zoning map and the zoning text.** The map tells you the district designation; the ordinance text tells you what that designation means. You need both.

**Watch for recent amendments.** Zoning ordinances get amended frequently. If the PDF you're reading looks dated, search for recent amendments to the specific article or district.

**Municipal vs. county jurisdiction matters.** A property in an incorporated city is governed by that city's zoning ordinance, not the county's — even if the county GIS shows a zoning designation. Confirm which jurisdiction has zoning authority.

**The SD/PUD trap.** Special district and planned unit development overlays sound developer-friendly because of their flexibility, but they also mean the planning commission has broad discretion to impose conditions. Flexibility cuts both ways. Always flag the discretionary risk.

**Density is not the same as what you can physically build.** The zoning density cap (e.g., 3,000 SF/unit = 14.5 units/acre on gross) is a legal maximum. Physical constraints (topography, stormwater, access, parking, setbacks) separately limit what fits on the site. Report both — the zoning ceiling and a practical estimate.

## Output Format

Present findings in a structured narrative — not a raw data dump. The audience is a developer or acquisitions professional who needs to make a go/no-go decision. Lead with the bottom line (can we build what we want here?), then support it with the regulatory detail.

If the user has specified a target program (e.g., "300 apartments and 100 townhomes"), evaluate that specific program against the zoning and tell them whether it works, what approvals are needed, and where the constraints bite.

## Before You Finalize — Run the Checklist

Before delivering your analysis, review `references/analyst-checklist.md`. It catalogs specific mistakes made on past deals and prompts to slow down and think harder on common failure modes — density math double-counting, source verification, scenario design, Excel model validation, and more. Not every check applies to every deal, but the act of going through the list surfaces issues that are easy to miss when you're moving quickly.

## Expanding This Skill

This skill is designed to grow as you underwrite more deals. After each analysis, consider:
- Did you encounter a zoning structure or ordinance format not covered here? Add a note to `references/zoning-patterns.md`
- Did a particular research technique work well (or fail)? Update the workflow steps
- Are there jurisdiction-specific quirks worth documenting? Add to `references/jurisdiction-notes.md`
- Did you make a mistake Jack had to correct? Add it to `references/analyst-checklist.md` under "Common Mistakes to Avoid" so you don't repeat it

references.name: analyst-checklist.md
references.content:
# Analyst Checklist — Things to Check Against

This is a catalog of mistakes made, judgment calls required, and questions to ask yourself during a deal analysis. These are not rigid rules. They are prompts to slow down and think harder before committing to an answer. If a check here doesn't apply to the specific deal, skip it — but make that a conscious choice, not a default.

## Scope & Clarification Checks

Before starting any analysis, have you confirmed:
- [ ] Depth the user wants (quick back-of-envelope vs. detailed deep-dive)
- [ ] Land price assumption (estimated from comps vs. listed vs. user-provided)
- [ ] Rent assumption (top of market vs. in-place comps vs. conservative vs. user-specified)
- [ ] Product type preference or open-ended
- [ ] Whether the user wants scenario comparison or a single recommendation

If multiple of these are ambiguous, ask before diving in. One AskUserQuestion at the start saves a rebuild later.

## Data Source Checks

- [ ] Zoning designation — verified against county/city GIS, not just the broker OM
- [ ] Zoning text — read from the actual ordinance, not a third-party summary
- [ ] Acreage — confirmed via GIS or deed, not taken from listing
- [ ] Jurisdiction — confirmed county vs. municipality (zoning authority differs)
- [ ] Rent comps — from actual property websites or rent surveys, not just Zillow/Apartments.com summaries
- [ ] Market data — from recent (< 12 months old) broker reports, not stale data

Broker OMs, listing sites, and aggregator data routinely get details wrong. Primary sources matter.

## Density Calculation Checks

- [ ] Does the zoning density standard apply to gross or net lot area? (Almost always gross.)
- [ ] Am I reporting the **zoning ceiling** (legal max units under the standard) separately from the **physical feasibility** (units per acre by product type)?
- [ ] Did I avoid multiplying gross acreage by an efficiency factor AND then dividing by per-unit minimum? (That's double-counting.)
- [ ] Does the district have different density standards for different product types (e.g., townhome by lot width vs. apartment by per-unit area)?
- [ ] Does an overlay/PD district modify or supersede the base district standards?
- [ ] What does the binding constraint actually say — the zoning ceiling or the physical fit? (Report both, identify which is binding.)

**The Oak Mountain Heights mistake:** I applied a 60% efficiency factor to the 24-acre site and then divided by 3,000 SF/unit to get 209 units — when the actual legal ceiling was 348 units. Jack correctly pushed back. The zoning standard doesn't care about net buildable area; it cares about parcel area. Physical feasibility is a separate check.

## Zoning Structure Checks

- [ ] Have I distinguished by-right uses from conditional uses (CUP/special exception)?
- [ ] Have I identified the approval body and process for any conditional approvals?
- [ ] Have I checked for overlay districts or special designations that modify the base zoning?
- [ ] For overlays: does the overlay supersede or supplement the base district? (Read the ordinance carefully — this varies.)
- [ ] Are there timeline/lapse provisions? (Some SD/PUD designations require construction commencement within 1-2 years.)
- [ ] Are there minimum site size requirements for overlays or specific product types?
- [ ] Does the district have different standards for renter-occupied vs. owner-occupied (rare but happens)?

## Dimensional Standard Checks

- [ ] Front, side, and rear setbacks captured
- [ ] Maximum height in both feet AND stories (sometimes the stricter wins)
- [ ] Parking requirements by bedroom count if differentiated
- [ ] Open space / landscape requirements (some districts require 10-20%)
- [ ] Lot coverage / FAR / impervious surface maxes
- [ ] Minimum unit size requirements (some jurisdictions impose floors)
- [ ] Buffer requirements adjacent to single-family or protected uses

## Feasibility Checks

- [ ] Does the height limit constrain product type? (35 ft = 3-story wood frame max; 45 ft = 4-story wood frame possible; 50+ ft opens mid-rise.)
- [ ] Does parking ratio constrain density below the zoning ceiling?
- [ ] Does the site have topography/environmental constraints that reduce buildable area beyond typical efficiency factors?
- [ ] Are there access/frontage issues that constrain site layout?
- [ ] Is the site in a floodplain, wetland, or conservation area?
- [ ] Is utility capacity available (water, sewer, power)?

## Scenario Design Checks

- [ ] For sites 15+ acres in flexible districts: have I considered mixed-product scenarios alongside single-product?
- [ ] For single-product scenarios: have I considered which product type actually fits the site and market best, or am I defaulting?
- [ ] Are rent assumptions defensible? (Top-of-market rents require top-of-market product; don't mix assumptions.)
- [ ] Are construction cost assumptions matched to the product and construction type? (Wood frame vs. podium vs. high-rise)
- [ ] Am I showing the zoning ceiling, the physical cap, and the proposed program separately — not conflating them?

## Construction Cost Tier Check

Construction costs vary by market, labor, and finish level, but as a starting benchmark for Sunbelt secondary markets:
- SFR / cottage / townhome (wood frame, 2-story): ~$140-170/SF
- Garden-style MF (wood frame, 3-story, surface parking): ~$160-175/SF
- Mid-rise MF (podium or light steel, 4-5 story): ~$190-215/SF
- High-rise / structured parking (steel/concrete, 6+ story): $250+/SF

Cost tier jumps at the construction type change, not linearly with height. If your scenario crosses 3 stories, flag the jump.

## Excel Model Checks

- [ ] Blue font for hardcoded inputs, black for formulas, yellow highlight for key assumptions (color coding convention)
- [ ] Zero formula errors after recalc (#REF!, #VALUE!, #DIV/0!, etc.)
- [ ] Cell references verified — not just absence of errors, but correct values
- [ ] Sample outputs sanity-checked (does the NOI, TDC, YOC look right for the product and market?)
- [ ] Unit count / rentable SF / rent assumptions cross-footed
- [ ] Per-unit and per-SF metrics reported alongside totals

## Output / Communication Checks

- [ ] Show the math — don't hide assumptions behind a number
- [ ] Cite sources for data points where possible (URL, document, date)
- [ ] Distinguish "this is what I know" from "this is my estimate"
- [ ] Lead with the bottom line (go/no-go, key ranges), support with detail
- [ ] Flag what I'm uncertain about — better to admit gaps than paper over them

## Process Checks

- [ ] For long analyses, am I managing context? (Break into chunks using deal-state if needed)
- [ ] Am I saving outputs to the right folder? (`C:\Users\jmiddleton\CWP\CWP - Documents\Ford\Dev Agent\deals\<deal-slug>\`)
- [ ] Am I updating skill references when I encounter new patterns or jurisdictions?

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

These are things I've actually gotten wrong, catalogued so I remember not to repeat them:

- **Double-counting site losses in density math** (Oak Mountain Heights — see density section above)
- **Defaulting to single-product scenarios without considering mixed product** on flexible, large sites
- **Conflating zoning ceiling with physical feasibility** — they're independent constraints
- **Hardcoding cell values in Excel instead of using formulas** — breaks the model for scenario testing
- **Referencing the wrong cells in formulas** — verify outputs after recalc, not just absence of errors
- **Reading broker-stated zoning without cross-checking against county ordinance** — OMs get zoning wrong
- **Treating SD/PUD flexibility as purely a positive** — discretionary approval cuts both ways; flag the risk
- **Skipping the clarifying questions step** — and then having to rebuild the analysis mid-way

references.name: jurisdiction-notes.md
references.content:
# Jurisdiction Notes

Jurisdiction-specific quirks, contacts, and research notes. Update after each deal in a new jurisdiction.

## Shelby County, Alabama (Unincorporated)

- **Zoning authority:** Shelby County Planning Commission
- **Ordinance source:** https://www.shelbyal.com/DocumentCenter/View/130/zoningregs_full
- **Format:** Single PDF, 113 pages, titled "Zoning Ordinance - Office Version Updated 4-02"
- **Structure:** Articles I-XXVII. Residential districts in Articles VII-XI (R-1 through R-4). Special districts in Article XVIII.
- **GIS:** Available at shelbyal.com (search "Shelby County Alabama GIS")
- **Notes:**
  - The SD overlay (Article XVIII) has three subsections: Section 1 (Generally), Section 2 (Planned Shopping Center — not allowed in R districts), Section 3 (Planned Residential), Section 4 (Planned Industrial — not allowed in R districts). For residential development, Section 3 is the operative provision.
  - The SD Planned Residential provisions effectively supersede base district dimensional standards subject to Planning Commission approval, which is unusual — many jurisdictions only allow minor modifications through overlay districts.
  - R-4 is the most permissive residential district. Multifamily is by-right; townhomes are conditional use in the base district but permitted by-right under the SD Planned Residential overlay.

references.name: zoning-patterns.md
references.content:
# Zoning Patterns Reference

This file captures zoning structures, ordinance formats, and research patterns encountered across deals. Update it after each new analysis.

## District Types Encountered

### R-4 Multiple Dwelling District (Shelby County, AL)
- **Ordinance:** Shelby County Zoning Ordinance, Article XI
- **Key features:** Permits single-family, two-family, and multifamily by right. Townhouses require conditional use approval via Planning Commission.
- **Density standard:** 3,000 SF minimum lot area per dwelling unit (applied to gross lot area for multifamily). Townhouses measured by lot width (20 ft/unit) rather than lot area.
- **Dimensional notes:** Separate standards for townhouses vs. other residential. Townhouse setbacks: 25 ft front/side(end)/rear, 35 ft max height. Apartment setbacks: 25 ft all yards, 45 ft max height.
- **Gotcha:** The 3,000 SF/unit standard applies to gross parcel area — don't net the site down first and then divide by 3,000.

### SD Special District — Planned Residential (Shelby County, AL)
- **Ordinance:** Shelby County Zoning Ordinance, Article XVIII, Section 3
- **Key features:** Overlay that requires Planning Commission site plan approval. Minimum 10-acre site. Permitted in any district except B and M.
- **Flexibility:** Base district minimum yard and maximum height requirements "shall not apply" except as noted in approved plan — gives PC discretion to vary dimensional standards.
- **Permitted uses:** Single-family (attached and detached), two-family, multifamily including condos and townhomes, limited commercial (4 acres per 100 units), recreation, educational, community facilities.
- **Required submissions:** Site development plan, drainage plan, deed restrictions, market study, traffic analysis.
- **Timeline risk:** Construction must commence within 1 year of zone amendment or PC can repeal. Must complete within 2 years of permit issuance.
- **Dimensional minimums:** 25-ft front yard (SFD), 25-ft general setback from ROW and project periphery, 5,000 SF min lot / 60-ft frontage for SFD lots, 10% permanent open space.
- **Key insight:** The SD overlay can actually increase density beyond base R-4 because it supersedes base dimensional standards subject to PC approval. But the discretionary process is both the opportunity and the risk.

## Research Patterns That Work

### Finding County Zoning Ordinances
- Search "[County name] zoning ordinance PDF" or "[County name] zoning regulations"
- County planning department websites usually host the full ordinance
- If not directly available, try the county's DocumentCenter (common for Shelby County-type setups)
- Municode and American Legal host many municipal codes — search "[City/County] municode"

### GIS / Parcel Viewers
- Search "[County name] GIS" or "[County name] parcel viewer" or "[County name] property search"
- Most counties now have online GIS with zoning layer overlays
- Cross-reference GIS zoning designation with broker-stated zoning — discrepancies are common

### When WebFetch Is Blocked
- Some county/government sites are blocked by egress restrictions
- Fallback: Use Chrome browser tools to navigate to the page and extract text via page reading tools
- For PDFs hosted on government sites, the browser PDF viewer works but page navigation can be clunky — use sidebar thumbnails and scroll rather than trying to jump to specific pages

## Density Calculation Cheat Sheet

| Product Type | Typical Gross Density | Construction Type | Typical Cost/SF | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Garden-style apartments (3-story) | 12-20 units/acre | Wood frame | $160-170/SF | Surface parking, most common suburban product |
| Mid-rise apartments (4-5 story) | 20-40 units/acre | Podium/steel + wood | $195-210/SF | Structured or tuck-under parking |
| Townhomes (attached, rental) | 8-14 units/acre | Wood frame | $150-160/SF | 20-24 ft wide typical, larger units (1,400-1,600 SF) |
| Single-family detached | 4-8 units/acre | Wood frame | $140-160/SF | Lot size dependent |
| Cottage-style SFR | 8-12 units/acre | Wood frame | $150-170/SF | Small lot, minimal setbacks |

These are physical density ranges — zoning may cap below these.

## Density Calculation Gotcha — Gross vs. Net

The zoning density standard (e.g., 3,000 SF/unit, or 14.5 units/acre) applies to the **gross lot area** of the parcel. This is a legal ceiling.

Physical site losses (roads, parking, stormwater, setbacks, open space, topography) are a **separate** constraint that determines what actually fits on the site.

**Do not double-count:** Multiplying gross acreage by an efficiency factor (e.g., 60%) and then applying the per-unit minimum is wrong — it understates the legal ceiling. Report the two constraints independently:
1. Zoning ceiling (gross area ÷ per-unit minimum)
2. Physical feasibility (product-type density range × gross acreage, sanity-checked against constraints)

Then take the binding constraint as your working maximum.

## Mixed-Product Scenario Pattern

Single-product scenarios often underperform mixed-product programs. Consider this default framework for sites 15+ acres in flexible districts (R-4, PUD, SD):

**Example: 24-acre site, R-4 SD, 348-unit ceiling**

| Component | Units | Avg SF | Rent PSF/Mo | Construction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rental townhomes | 100 | 1,500 | $1.75 | Wood frame, $155/SF |
| Garden MF (3-story) | 100 | 1,000 | $2.00 | Wood frame, $165/SF |
| Mid-rise MF (4-story) | 148 | 1,000 | $2.20 | Podium, $200/SF |

This blended program delivered ~6.81% YOC vs. ~5.67% YOC for a single-product 188-unit garden-only scenario on the same site. The mid-rise carries a cost premium but pulls higher rent PSF, and the townhome rental product commands meaningful monthly rent ($2,625) that diversifies the revenue profile.

**When mixed product works best:**
- Site has terrain variation (townhomes on slopes, mid-rise on flat areas)
- Market supports multiple price points
- SD/PUD overlay permits multiple product types in one plan
- Absorption risk benefits from phased delivery across product types

memories.name: Analyst Process Habits
memories.description: Cross-deal habits for underwriting work — clarifying questions first, primary sources over broker data, show the math, separate zoning from physical feasibility
memories.content:
Habits that should apply across deals (not specific to any one site):

**Clarifying questions first.** Before any underwriting analysis, confirm: depth expected (quick back-of-envelope vs. detailed), land price source, rent assumption framework (top-of-market vs. conservative vs. in-place), and product preference. One AskUserQuestion up front saves a mid-analysis rebuild.

**Primary sources over broker summaries.** Broker OMs, Zillow, Apartments.com, and listing sites routinely get zoning, acreage, and rent data wrong. Verify against county GIS, ordinance text, and actual property sources. This applies to zoning and market data both.

**Show the math.** Jack will push back if logic is hidden or math is sloppy. Better to expose assumptions openly — including the ones I'm unsure about — than to present a clean number without reasoning.

**Report zoning ceiling and physical feasibility separately.** These are independent constraints. The zoning density standard is a legal cap applied to gross parcel area. Physical feasibility (units/acre by product type, sanity-checked against site constraints) is a separate check. Don't conflate them into a single conservative number.

**Why:** These are recurring patterns observed across the Oak Mountain Heights analysis — each one came up as a correction or preference Jack expressed. They're not rules to follow rigidly; they're habits that lead to better work. When a specific deal argues against one of these (e.g., the user has already provided clear scope, so clarifying questions aren't needed), skip — but make that a conscious call.

**How to apply:** At the start of each new deal analysis, scan this list. Are any of these habits at risk of being skipped? If so, correct before proceeding. Reference `references/analyst-checklist.md` in the zoning-analysis skill for the fuller catalog of things to check.

memories.name: Context Window Management
memories.description: Break long underwriting processes into chunks using deal-state.json as shared memory between context windows
memories.content:
Jack explicitly wants the underwriting broken into manageable chunks so the agent doesn't hit context limits and quit mid-analysis. Each step should be independently completable.

**Why:** A thorough underwriting with real research (web searches, GIS lookups, comp analysis) burns a lot of context. Trying to do all 9 steps in one window risks truncation or degraded quality toward the end.

**How to apply:** Use deal-state.json as the handoff mechanism. Each sub-skill reads the state, does its step, writes outputs back. The orchestrator (deal-analyst) can then reset context and pick up the next step. The database also serves as persistent memory across sessions — always save reusable data (comps, MSA stats, tax rates) to the DB so future deals benefit.

memories.name: Density Math Precision
memories.description: Zoning density standards apply to gross lot area — do not double-count site losses by applying an efficiency factor and then dividing by per-unit minimum
memories.content:
When calculating maximum units under a zoning density standard (e.g., 3,000 SF/unit), apply it to the gross lot area of the parcel. Do not first reduce the site by an efficiency factor (e.g., × 60% for roads/parking/open space) and then divide by the per-unit minimum — that double-counts the losses and understates the legal ceiling.

**Why:** I made this exact mistake on Oak Mountain Heights (24 acres, R-4, 3,000 SF/unit). I calculated 1,045,440 × 0.60 ÷ 3,000 = 209 units as a "practical ceiling" when the legal ceiling was actually 348 units. Jack correctly pushed back — the zoning standard doesn't care about net buildable area; it cares about parcel area. Physical constraints are a separate check.

**How to apply:** Always report two numbers independently when doing density analysis:
1. **Zoning ceiling:** gross acreage × 43,560 ÷ per-unit minimum SF
2. **Physical feasibility:** gross acreage × typical units/acre range for the product type

Then identify which is the binding constraint. Don't conflate them into a single conservative number — it hides real development potential and causes Jack to lose trust in the math.

memories.name: Expense Underwriting Methodology
memories.description: How to underwrite operating expenses — statistical models from T12 database for controllables/insurance, tax comps for property tax, 2.35% of net operating revenue for management
memories.content:
The T12 database is NOT CWP's portfolio. It is a collection of T12s from acquisition candidates — properties CWP has evaluated. Do not treat the data as "our properties" or take simple averages.

**How to use the T12 database:**
Build statistical models that predict what the subject property's expenses will be based on property characteristics (unit count, product type, year built, geography, etc.). Don't just average — regress or segment.

**Specific expense underwriting rules:**
- **Controllable expenses:** Underwrite using statistical models derived from the T12 database. Segment by relevant property characteristics.
- **Insurance:** Same approach — statistical model from T12 data.
- **Property tax:** Do NOT use the T12 database. Use property tax comps — look up assessed values from GIS/county tax assessor portals for comparable properties. The zoning-analysis skill references have notes on how to pull these.
- **Management fee:** 2.35% × Net Operating Revenue (NOR). Note: NOR, not EGI. This is a specific distinction Jack made.

**Why:** Simple averages across a heterogeneous set of acquisition candidates don't predict anything useful. A 140-unit property in Virginia Beach has different cost structure than a 484-unit property in Charlotte. The model needs to account for these differences.

**How to apply:** When running Step 5 (NOI Underwriting), build the expense estimate from the statistical model, not from averages. Flag the model's confidence interval. For property taxes, always use local comps from the GIS/assessor portal.

memories.name: Flexible Backend Approach
memories.description: Don't preemptively add schema fields to the worker — use JSON blob columns and let Jack drive what gets added
memories.content:
Don't preemptively add fields or endpoints to the pipeline-backend worker. Jack explicitly said: "you arent going to be able to preemptively add everything i am going to want in the backend so you should stop trying."

**Why:** The underwriting system's needs evolve as deals progress and new analysis types get built. Preemptive schema additions waste time and often miss what's actually needed.

**How to apply:** 
- Use TEXT columns with JSON blobs (like `entitlement_data`) for extensible data storage
- Only add new fields/endpoints when Jack specifically asks for them or when a concrete feature requires it
- The worker already supports `entitlement_data` as a JSON blob — this pattern works well
- When building new analysis features, store results in deal-state.json locally and only push to the API when Jack confirms the data structure

memories.name: Insurance Model Drivers
memories.description: Insurance should be modeled primarily by product type, rentable square footage, age, and state — not just units and rent
memories.content:
Insurance expense models should use these features as primary drivers:
1. **Product type** (garden vs. BTR/cottage vs. mid-rise/wrap)
2. **Rentable square footage** (avg SF per unit)
3. **Age** of the property (years since built)
4. **State** (catastrophe risk varies — FL, TX higher)

Unit count is secondary. Avg rent should NOT be a primary feature for insurance — it's the physical characteristics and location risk that drive premiums.

**Why:** Jack specified these features explicitly. Insurance underwriting is about the physical risk profile of the building, not its revenue.

**How to apply:** When building or updating the insurance regression model, ensure avg_sf, age, product_type dummies, and state dummies are the primary features. Current model (R²=0.551, n=18) uses these features. Check expense-models.json for latest coefficients.

memories.name: Consider Mixed Product Scenarios
memories.description: Don't default to single-product analyses; pause and consider whether the site characteristics and zoning support a mixed-product program that might deliver better returns
memories.content:
Before locking into a single-product underwriting scenario (all garden MF, all townhomes, all SFR), pause and ask whether the site and zoning context make a mixed-product program worth modeling alongside it.

**Why:** On Oak Mountain Heights, I defaulted to a 188-unit garden-only baseline. Jack pushed to model a 348-unit three-product program (100 TH + 100 garden + 148 mid-rise) which delivered ~6.81% YOC vs. ~5.67% YOC. The mixed-product answer was materially better, but only because this specific site supported it — flexible SD zoning, enough acreage (24 acres), and market depth at multiple price points. That doesn't mean mixed product is always better; it means I should surface it as an option when the fact pattern supports it.

**How to apply:** When evaluating a site, consider mixed-product if:
- Site is 15+ acres (small sites usually don't support multiple products)
- Zoning permits multiple residential product types (either by right or through overlay/PD)
- Topography varies (slopes favor townhomes/SFR, flats favor higher-density MF)
- Market has genuine depth at multiple price points (not just assumed)
- Phased absorption risk is a concern

If those conditions are present, model at least one mixed-product scenario and let Jack compare. If they're not present, a single-product scenario is usually the right answer and don't force mixed product just for its own sake.

memories.name: Show Your Work
memories.description: Jack requires every data point to have source URLs, hyperlinks, and confidence levels — no unsourced numbers
memories.content:
Every data point in a deal analysis must have a traceable source. Jack wants to be able to click into any number and see where it came from.

**Why:** Jack needs to audit the analysis and refine the process. Unsourced numbers are untrusted. He also wants the agent to be able to audit itself.

**How to apply:** Always include source_urls in deal-state.json. When reporting comps, assessed values, tax rates, etc., include the hyperlink. Use the data_confidence field (gold/good/estimate) in the database. Flag estimates explicitly as due diligence items rather than presenting them as facts.

memories.name: Dev Agent Underwriting System
memories.description: Multi-skill real estate underwriting system with SQLite database, deal-state JSON memory, and 10 sub-skills for context-window-safe deal analysis
memories.content:
The CWP Development Agent is a multi-step underwriting system built April 2026. It breaks the 9-step deal analysis into independent sub-skills that share state via deal-state.json files.

**Why:** A single context window can't handle thorough research + analysis for all 9 steps. The system lets each step run independently, persist its outputs, and hand off to the next step.

**How to apply:** When a new deal comes in, create a deal folder from the template, then run each sub-skill in sequence. Check the database before web searching. Save reusable data back to the database after each step.

Key components:
- SQLite DB at `Dev Agent/database/cwp_dev_agent.db` (12 tables)
- DB utilities at `Dev Agent/database/db_utils.py`
- Deal template at `Dev Agent/deals/_template/deal-state.json`
- Sub-skills at `Dev Agent/skills/` (site-analysis, market-rent-comps, unit-mix-program, noi-underwriting, dev-cost-estimate, financing-analysis, returns-feasibility, strategy-recommendation)
- Architecture doc at `Dev Agent/system/architecture.md`
- Needs-from-Jack memo at `Dev Agent/system/needs-from-jack.md`

Pending: SharePoint T12 import, Notion tax rate import, rms.cwprop.com pipeline page integration, git backup, HelloData/CoStar access.

memories.name: CWP Company Files
memories.description: Full CWP company shared drive mounted — Acquisitions, Development, Owned Assets, Strategy, Admin, etc.
memories.content:
CWP's full company shared drive is at `C:\Users\jmiddleton\CWP\CWP - Documents` (mounted as `/sessions/exciting-nice-hamilton/mnt/CWP - Documents`).

Top-level folders: Acquisitions, Admin, Controlled Access, Development, Ford, General, Organizational Documents, Owned Assets, Strategy, x. Claude

The T12 database was previously in the Acquisitions subfolder. Other company data (deal files, OMs, budgets, etc.) should be accessible here.

**When to use:** When Jack references company documents, prior deal analysis, OMs, budgets, or any internal CWP files — check this directory first before asking for uploads.

memories.name: Deployment Workflow
memories.description: How to deploy pipeline frontend and backend — GitHub Actions auto-deploy, Cloudflare credentials, Chrome workaround for sandbox egress
memories.content:
**Frontend (pipeline.cwprop.com):**
- Auto-deploys via GitHub Actions on push to `main` with `frontend/**` path changes
- Workflow: `.github/workflows/deploy-frontend.yml` in `fordcwprop/claude-cowork`
- Uses `cloudflare/wrangler-action@v3` with `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN` secret
- Cloudflare Pages project: `pipeline-frontend`
- Can also trigger manually via `workflow_dispatch`

**Backend (pipeline-backend worker):**
- Deployed via Cloudflare dashboard code editor (no GitHub Actions yet)
- Use Chrome browser → Cloudflare dashboard → Workers → pipeline-backend → Edit Code
- Find & Replace is efficient for targeted changes
- Worker version as of 2026-04-09: d5bcd324

**Credentials:**
- Cloudflare Account ID: ea21224737d86d6da54d6b78c173141b
- D1 Database: pipeline-production (cf13813d-38f2-4202-81dc-57c745054f23)
- GitHub repo: fordcwprop/claude-cowork
- Git config: user.email "macminicp@gmail.com", user.name "CW Dev Agent (Cowork)"

**Sandbox limitations:**
- Direct API calls to GitHub/Cloudflare from sandbox are blocked (403 tunnel error)
- Workaround: Use Chrome browser automation for dashboard interactions
- Cloudflare dashboard internal API proxy works when logged in via Chrome

memories.name: Reference: Dev Agent Repo + Cowork Backup Mechanism
memories.description: fordcwprop/dev-agent private repo backs up skills/deals/DB-scripts; Cowork can push directly via PAT + scratch-clone workaround
memories.content:
## The repo

**GitHub:** `fordcwprop/dev-agent` (private)
**Source folder on Jack's machine:** `C:\Users\jmiddleton\CWP\CWP - Documents\Ford\Dev Agent\`
**Sandbox mount:** `/sessions/<session-id>/mnt/Dev Agent/`

Contents tracked: `skills/`, `deals/`, `database/` (scripts only, not the `.db` file), `system/`, top-level `.md` docs, `backup.ps1`, `.gitignore`, `README.md`. See `.gitignore` for exclusions (SQLite DB, __pycache__, .skill zips, office lock files, `.github-pat`).

## How to push from Cowork (REST API won't work, use scratch-clone workaround)

**Why the scratch clone:**
- `api.github.com` is blocked by sandbox egress allowlist (verified: HTTP 403 from proxy)
- `github.com` itself IS reachable, so git-over-HTTPS works
- Direct git operations on the mount (`/mnt/Dev Agent`) fail with "Operation not permitted" because Windows/SharePoint ACLs don't allow the sandbox to delete/modify existing files
- Solution: clone to a sandbox scratch dir (fully writable), rsync the working tree from the mount, commit and push from there

**The workflow:**

```bash
# 1. Read PAT (stored at mount root, gitignored)
TOKEN=$(cat "/sessions/<sid>/mnt/Dev Agent/.github-pat" | tr -d '[:space:]')

# 2. Clone (or pull if already cloned this session)
mkdir -p /sessions/<sid>/work
cd /sessions/<sid>/work
git clone "https://x-access-token:${TOKEN}@github.com/fordcwprop/dev-agent.git"
cd dev-agent

# 3. CRITICAL git configs (avoid false diffs from Windows mount)
git config core.autocrlf input       # normalize CRLF → LF
git config core.filemode false       # ignore exec-bit flapping (mount shows everything as 755)
git config user.email "jmiddleton@cwprop.com"
git config user.name "Cowork Auto-Backup"

# 4. Sync working tree from mount, excluding non-tracked items
rsync -av --delete \
  --exclude='.git/' \
  --exclude='.git-broken-*' \
  --exclude='.github-pat' \
  --exclude='__pycache__/' \
  --exclude='*.pyc' \
  --exclude='*.db' \
  --exclude='*.db-journal' \
  --exclude='*.skill' \
  --exclude='.backup.log' \
  --exclude='.~lock.*#' \
  --exclude='~$*' \
  "/sessions/<sid>/mnt/Dev Agent/" ./

# 5. Commit and push
git add -A
git diff --cached --quiet || git commit -m "Auto-backup YYYY-MM-DD: <summary of changes>"
git push origin main
```

## The PAT

- Stored at: `C:\Users\jmiddleton\CWP\CWP - Documents\Ford\Dev Agent\.github-pat` (in SharePoint — CWP internal only)
- Gitignored: yes (`.github-pat` line in `.gitignore`)
- Scope: fine-grained, `fordcwprop/dev-agent` only, Contents: read+write, Metadata: read-only
- Created: 2026-04-13

## Safety net: Windows Task Scheduler daily job

Separate daily backup via `backup.ps1` script in the repo root. Registered on Jack's machine as "CWP Dev Agent Backup" scheduled task, runs at 3 AM daily. Uses his cached Windows GitHub credentials (not the PAT). This catches any changes Jack makes outside of Cowork sessions.

## Known quirks

- `.git-broken-*` folders may appear in the mount from failed init attempts; sandbox can't delete them, just exclude in rsync
- LibreOffice/Excel lock files (`.~lock.*#`, `~$*.xlsx`) show up when Jack has a deal Excel open — already excluded
- First Windows push committed `.git-broken-*` and a `.~lock.*#` file into the repo; cleaned up in commit `f7eb267`

memories.name: Notion Database
memories.description: Notion database tracks property tax rates, proffers, tap fees, and impact fees by locality — check before researching
memories.content:
Jack tracks locality-specific financial data in a Notion database: property tax rates, proffers, water/sewer tap fees, impact fees, and similar jurisdiction-level costs. The agent should check this database before researching these items from scratch.

Status as of April 2026: Not yet connected. Need either a Notion export or MCP connector access from Jack.

memories.name: Pipeline API (pipeline.cwprop.com)
memories.description: CWP deal pipeline API at pipeline-backend.office-a21.workers.dev — POST/PATCH deals, auto-calculates NOI/IRR/DSCR/cap rates
memories.content:
CWP's deal pipeline is at pipeline.cwprop.com with a backend API at pipeline-backend.office-a21.workers.dev.

Auth header: `Cf-Access-Authenticated-User-Email: macminicp@gmail.com`

Key endpoints: POST /api/deals (create), PATCH /api/deals/{id} (update), GET /api/deals (list), GET /api/deals/{id} (single with metrics).

The API auto-calculates: NOI, cap rate, DSCR, price/unit, cash-on-cash, levered IRR, equity multiple, yield on cost, risk score.

Status values: new, reviewing, underwriting, loi, due-diligence, closed, dead.

Full field reference saved at Dev Agent/system/pipeline-api-reference.md.

**Access note (April 2026):** The API domain is blocked by Cowork's sandbox egress allowlist. Use Chrome browser tools or have Jack run curl commands locally as workaround. Or request allowlist addition.

memories.name: rms.cwprop.com Pipeline Website
memories.description: CWP's pipeline website at rms.cwprop.com — needs codebase access to add deal maps, strategy pages, and skill visibility features
memories.content:
rms.cwprop.com is CWP's internal pipeline/deal management website. Jack wants several features added:
- Display agent skills/memory on the pipeline page (synced with internal files)
- Market strategy map with blue/green/yellow zones and deal dots
- Tooltip drill-downs showing underlying data and assumptions for every metric
- Strategy page listing all market strategies with the agent's code/logic
- Nightly sync to keep pipeline page current with agent's analysis

The codebase has its own git backup process that Jack wants replicated for the Dev Agent system.

Status as of April 2026: No codebase access yet. Need the repo URL or folder mount from Jack.

memories.name: SharePoint T12 Database
memories.description: Excel T12 database on SharePoint contains historical income/expense data for expense benchmarking — needs import to SQLite
memories.content:
Jack has an Excel database of T12 (trailing 12 month) income and expense statements on SharePoint. This is the primary source for:
- Other income ratios (% of GPR)
- Controllable expense ratios
- Per-unit expense benchmarks
- Insurance and payroll benchmarks

Status as of April 2026: Not yet imported. Need the file from Jack to parse and load into the t12_data and expense_benchmarks tables in SQLite.

memories.name: Jack's Profile
memories.description: Jack is a principal at CWP, a multifamily and BTR developer in the Southeast US targeting 250+ unit communities
memories.content:
Jack is the principal/decision-maker at CWP (cwprop.com). He evaluates multifamily apartment and build-to-rent (BTR) townhome/cottage development opportunities in the Southeast US. Target deal size is 250+ units.

He's technically sophisticated — comfortable with SQL databases, code, git, and building systems. He previously used an "OpenClaw agent" for some of this work. He thinks in terms of systems and wants visibility into the agent's process so he can audit and refine it.

He values: directness, proof of work (show sources), efficiency (don't waste tokens), and institutional knowledge building (database gets smarter over time). He wants the agent to flag what it doesn't know rather than papering over gaps.

Communication style: casual, direct, uses "you" to address the agent. Okay with technical terminology. Doesn't need hand-holding but wants to see the reasoning.
